sections.body_html:
Where we are: The semiconductor supply chain has a sequence of physical constraints that limit how fast AI can scale. Each one gets solved eventually, but the next one becomes the new ceiling. Right now, GPU cloud demand and N3 logic wafers and Power / DC buildout and Hyperscaler capex pull are the active constraints — they're happening simultaneously, not sequentially as originally expected.

What the money says: Leopold Aschenbrenner's fund ($5.5B) and Gavin Baker's fund ($8.2B) are both betting heavily on AI infrastructure but disagree on where the constraint is tightest. Leopold has exited chip companies entirely and loaded up on power infrastructure and GPU cloud operators. Baker has done the opposite — $1B+ in Nvidia alone — betting that chips themselves remain scarce. The cleanest three-source overlap is optical interconnect (CRWV), which makes it the highest-conviction, lowest-divergence-risk zone in the entire thesis.
sections.title: Summary

rows.bottleneck_role: Primary read-through on downstream AI infrastructure demand. CoreWeave is not the upstream bottleneck itself; it is the proof that GPU cloud demand is strong enough to keep pulling on chips, memory, optics, and power simultaneously.
break_conditions: Customer demand or backlog conversion weakens enough to expose the leverage.
Interest expense and capex stay so high that scale fails to turn into margin.
GPU availability improves while CoreWeave still cannot earn through the balance sheet.
rows.confirm_signal: Delivered 50K GB200s in Q4 to single customer. Nvidia GPUs 'remain in short supply' per Intrator. Massive capex plan ($30-35B) = enormous chip demand.
confirms_next: Q1 2026 revenue lands inside the $1.9B-$2.0B guide and operating income avoids a deeper trough.
Backlog, power capacity, or reserved-instance commentary keeps moving up rather than flattening.
Rubin deployment timing and lower financing costs show up as visible execution progress in 2026.

due_claims.claim: Q1 represents the trough in annual margin trajectory — margins ramp from low single digits in Q1, expanding each quarter, to low double-digits by Q4 2026
We expect to continue reducing our weighted average cost of capital

data_points: AWS p6-b200 spot: $14/hr/GPU
Month-on-month rental increases 15-20% (Jan-Mar 2026)
All new capacity through Aug-Sep 2026 already booked
Blackwell deployment lead times extended to Jun-Jul 2026
semi_signals.title: The Great GPU Shortage — Rental Capacity

data_points: Big tech total 2026 AI spending estimated around $650B, +80% YoY
Google and Amazon remain the two largest incremental demand engines for accelerator clusters
Cloud procurement cadence supports sustained NVDA and CRWV order visibility
semi_signals.title: Hyperscaler CapEx Pull Through

rows.why_now: Q4 2025 revenue reached $1.57B, backlog expanded to $66.8B, management said it still could not catch up with demand, and virtually all 2026 capacity was already allocated. That makes CoreWeave the strongest current demand validator in the repo.

rows.bottleneck_role: Core consumer and monetizer of the active semiconductor bottlenecks. NVIDIA does not just benefit from scarcity; its own roadmap and shipment volume are what make N3, memory, and optics binding.
break_conditions: Rubin slips or Blackwell-to-Rubin transitions slow enough to weaken supply pressure.
Networking growth stalls, undermining the optics read-through.
China restrictions or competitor catch-up force margin compression before FY2027.
rows.confirm_signal: Rubin moving to N3, competing with TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X for TSMC capacity. $95.2B supply commitments (nearly doubled QoQ) = enormous wafer demand. Vera Rubin production H2 2026 adds further N3 pressure. Six new chips this cycle — all need N3.
confirms_next: Q1 FY2027 reaches the $78B +/-2% guide with margins still in the mid-70s.
Rubin production timing, supply commitments, and networking growth stay on track through 2026.
Inventory and customer backlog keep supporting the $500B visibility claim.

due_claims.claim: Mid-70s gross margins sustainable through FY2027

guidance.notes: Starting Q1, SBC included in non-GAAP results

data_points: AWS p6-b200 spot: $14/hr/GPU
Month-on-month rental increases 15-20% (Jan-Mar 2026)
All new capacity through Aug-Sep 2026 already booked
Blackwell deployment lead times extended to Jun-Jul 2026
semi_signals.title: The Great GPU Shortage — Rental Capacity

data_points: AI = 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027
Reallocating 5% smartphone N3 wafers = ~100K Rubin GPUs or ~300K TPU v7s
Google 2026 capex roughly doubled vs prior expectations
Amazon 2026 AI infrastructure spend remains on a steep expansion path
All major AI accelerators moving to N3 simultaneously
semi_signals.title: The Great AI Silicon Shortage

rows.why_now: Q4 FY2026 printed $68.1B revenue, $11B of networking revenue, and $95.2B of supply commitments, while management guided another step up to $78B in Q1 and pushed Rubin production into H2 2026. That combination keeps NVIDIA at the center of the current cascade.

rows.bottleneck_role: Foundational logic bottleneck. If leading-edge wafer capacity is tight, the entire AI stack backs up. If TSMC overbuilds or demand softens, the shortage starts to break.
break_conditions: Leading-edge demand cools enough to pull revenue/margins below guide.
Massive capex results in faster-than-expected supply relief.
AI customers digest inventory and node mix stops moving up.
rows.confirm_signal: 3nm already accounted for 28% of wafer revenue, and 7nm-and-below advanced nodes were 77% of total wafer revenue, showing AI demand remains concentrated in constrained leading-edge supply.
confirms_next: Q1 2026 lands near the $34.6B-$35.8B guide with margins staying above 63%.
2026 USD revenue growth remains close to 30%.
Advanced-node mix keeps rising rather than flattening.
rows.contradict_signal: A 2026 capital budget of $52B-$56B is a real supply response. It does not break the shortage today, but it is exactly the kind of capex that can eventually ease logic tightness.

data_points: AWS p6-b200 spot: $14/hr/GPU
Month-on-month rental increases 15-20% (Jan-Mar 2026)
All new capacity through Aug-Sep 2026 already booked
Blackwell deployment lead times extended to Jun-Jul 2026

data_points: AI = 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027

Google 2026 capex roughly doubled vs prior expectations
Amazon 2026 AI infrastructure spend remains on a steep expansion path
All major AI accelerators moving to N3 simultaneously

rows.why_now: Q4 2025 showed continued strong leading-edge demand, 3nm already at 28% of wafer revenue, advanced nodes at 77%, and another strong guide into Q1 2026 alongside a $52B-$56B capex plan.
